```python

self.run_command('egg_info')
```

Is it because `run_command` and `register.run` generate a bunch of text, and you want the deprecation warning to be printed *after* this text?

```
try:

    orig.register.run(self)
except Exception:
    raise
finally:
    self._deprecation_notice()
```

There's one annoying issue (MSVC related, as usual) with doing it that way: MSVC considers all the constexpr variables unused if only used in the `static_assert`, and so issues a warning which breaks the build, and so separate variables would need a `(void) (compiletime_bases, runtime_bases, needs_mi);` to silence it, which is a bit ugly.

I thought it might also end up being potentially less work for the compiler this way: typically the number of base classes is 0, so short-circuiting avoids needing to compute everything but the first one.

I'm not happy with all this being moved from resource.py to client_resource.py.  The design idea is:

resource.py specifies the abstract interface of what a KATCP resource is supposed to look like
client_resource.py specifies an implementation that uses direct KATCP connections.

The nice thing about having abstract classes is also that certain functionalities can be kept abstract in that they only depend on the implementors implementing a certain subset of the whole abstract API, see for instance the ABCs in python's builting collections module.

Personally, I think having the default be "infinity" epochs might do the right thing here, but I'm not familiar enough with how `get_num_batches` is used yet to say if it'd create bugs.

I'd slightly prefer that you do these operations in the tree construction in `torch` rather than numpy, just because it results in less conversion clutter, but I can change this afterward if you prefer numpy.

The other option is to make this optional independent of whether you also have linking features (e.g., if you want to use both linking features and this kind of neighbor similarity).  Probably not worth the additional complexity, though, at this point.

The main reason I did it is for test isolation. I tend to always use "failfast" when running tests and when I realized that I couldn't run the test again because my daemon was still in Swarm mode, I realized that enter/leave should actually be done systematically to avoid being in an inconsistent state.

It makes all sense imho: a Docker in Swarm mode is something very different to handle than a simple Docker daemon:
1. you need to join/leave the swarm
2. you get access to the "services" API

So in my opinion it is so much clearer to have a fixture "swarm" for a Docker Swarm environment and "docker" for a simple Docker environment.

It provides also a few perks:
1. If you now run the tests on your own Docker instance, if the daemon is already in swarm mode, it will not work. I can imagine that people who run tests doesn't want the test to remove all their services.
2. If you are not in Swarm mode, it will give you back your Docker daemon in the state it was before running the tests because it leaves the Swarm no matter what.

So now why in `conftest.py` and not in `test_services.py`? Well, we could test other stuff of a Swarm than just the services.

This will -- or should -- be the package name used by NPM too. So it's better to be consistent with other languages. If it's `google-cloud-iam` package in other languages, then it will be `@google-cloud/iam`. If they are `google-cloud-iam-admin`, it's better to be `@google-cloud/iam-admin`.

Note that NPM package names are not hierarchical at all. Normally package name can't contain `/` at all, but a format of `@(org name)/(package name)` is allowed (and `org name` still can't contain `/`).

It's a bit confusion because the usage scenario is a bit confusion.  When dev_depedency is set to True, all we really do is not to include this module in class path (or bundlable js product) so that it does not show up in the final bundle.  Its dependent node_module targets are treated separately based on their target definition.  

The reason is sometimes developers writes a build time tool in JS.  They might include this build tool as a dependency.  An example is `contrib/node/examples/src/node/web-component-button`.  It includes `contrib/node/examples/src/node/web-build-tool` as a dependency.  web-build-tool simply invokes webpack with some customized configuration included. The example is simplified.   A more realistic example is a node_module includes a JS tool for asset uploading or template hydration.
I don't think we support this kind of usage in other languages as this is usage pattern is only common for Node developers.

There has been a fair amount of back-and-forth on whether or not it's even desirable to disable the submit button, so I'm inclined to lean towards (2) with a slight amendment to the 'Submission' section of the forms spec:

* Remove: _If there is a field that is clearly marked with an error on the client side, the submit button for the form should be disabled until the client side errors on each affected field are gone._
* Add: _If the submit button is clicked and there are visible client-side errors, set keyboard focus in the first field that contains an error_
